questions.question: What is the defining property that makes a collection of components a 'system'?
options: They share the same codebase
They produce collective behavior not achievable by any single component alone
They all run on the same server
They are all written in the same programming language
questions.explanation: A system is defined by emergent behavior: interacting components producing collective outcomes that no single component could achieve alone. This is why systems thinking matters — the interactions matter as much as the components.

questions.question: What is the 'local optimization trap' in system architecture?
options: Using local variables instead of global ones
Teams optimizing individual components that degrade overall system performance
Optimizing database queries without indexing
Caching data that changes frequently
questions.explanation: The local optimization trap occurs when teams optimize their individual components in isolation, but the interactions between components degrade system-level behavior. Example: aggressive caching reducing per-service latency but creating thundering herd problems at the system level.

questions.question: Which type of feedback loop drives amplification — where a change in one direction causes more change in the same direction?
options: Negative (balancing) loop
Neutral loop
Positive (reinforcing) loop
Dampening loop
questions.explanation: Positive (reinforcing) feedback loops amplify change in the same direction. Example: more load → slower responses → more timeouts → more retries → more load. This is why retry storms and cascading failures are so dangerous.

questions.question: A circuit breaker is an example of which type of feedback mechanism?
options: Positive (reinforcing) feedback
Negative (balancing) feedback
Open-loop control
Chaotic feedback
questions.explanation: A circuit breaker is negative (balancing) feedback: as failures increase, the circuit opens → load on the downstream service decreases → it recovers → the circuit closes. It counteracts the failure signal rather than amplifying it.

questions.question: What is Conway's Law and how does it relate to software architecture?
options: Systems are always more complex than expected
Organizations design systems that mirror their own communication structure
All software has bugs proportional to its size
Distributed systems fail in proportion to their node count
questions.explanation: Conway's Law: 'Organizations design systems that mirror their own communication structure.' The Inverse Conway Maneuver is to structure teams around the desired architecture first — team boundaries naturally produce matching service boundaries.

questions.question: What is a 'distributed monolith' anti-pattern?
options: A monolith deployed across multiple data centers
Services that must be deployed together, share a database, and have deep synchronous chains — all distributed complexity with none of the benefits
A microservice that is too large and needs to be split
Running the same monolith on multiple servers for scaling
questions.explanation: A distributed monolith has all the operational complexity of microservices (network calls, distributed failures) without any of the benefits (independent deployability, isolated failures). Services deploy together, share state, and have tight coupling.

questions.question: What does the CQRS pattern separate?
options: Code and configuration
Write model (commands) from read model (queries), allowing independent optimization
Client from server logic
Synchronous from asynchronous processing
questions.explanation: CQRS (Command Query Responsibility Segregation) separates the write model (optimized for consistency and integrity) from the read model (optimized for query speed). This enables different storage engines, independent scaling, and specialized optimization for each side.

questions.question: What does Event Sourcing store as the source of truth?
options: The current state snapshot of each entity
An immutable log of all events that occurred, from which current state is derived
Aggregated statistics updated on each write
A changelog of database schema migrations
questions.explanation: Event Sourcing stores an immutable sequence of events (e.g., 'OrderPlaced', 'PaymentReceived'). Current state is derived by replaying events. This enables temporal queries ('what was the state at time T?') and a complete audit trail.

questions.question: What is the key difference between vertical scaling and horizontal scaling?
options: Vertical = more servers; Horizontal = bigger servers
Vertical = bigger/faster single machine; Horizontal = adding more machines
Vertical works only for databases; Horizontal for web servers
They are the same with different names
questions.explanation: Vertical scaling (scale up) means giving a single machine more CPU, RAM, or disk. Horizontal scaling (scale out) means adding more machines. Vertical hits physical limits with exponential cost curves; horizontal adds distributed systems complexity.

questions.question: Amdahl's Law states that if 10% of a workload is sequential (non-parallelizable), the maximum theoretical speedup from adding more CPUs is:
options: Unlimited
2x
questions.explanation: Amdahl's Law: max speedup = 1 / (sequential_fraction + (1 - sequential_fraction) / N). If 10% is sequential, max speedup = 1/0.1 = 10x, regardless of how many parallel processors you add. Sequential bottlenecks fundamentally cap parallelism.

questions.question: Arrange the circuit breaker states in order from normal operation through failure and recovery:
questions.explanation: Circuit breaker states: CLOSED (normal, counting failures) → OPEN (failure threshold exceeded, all requests rejected immediately) → HALF_OPEN (probe request sent to test recovery) → back to CLOSED if probe succeeds.

questions.question: In a distributed system with 10,000 servers at 99.9% uptime each, the probability of all servers being healthy simultaneously is very high.
questions.explanation: False. The probability = 0.999^10000 ≈ 0.005% (essentially zero). Continuous partial failure is the normal operating condition at scale — something is always failing. Resilient systems are designed to tolerate partial failures, not prevent them.

questions.question: What is the 'blast radius' concept in resilience engineering?
options: The physical distance data can travel in a network
The scope of users affected when a component fails, measured by (affected users / total) × duration
The number of servers destroyed in a hardware failure
The latency increase caused by a cascading failure
questions.explanation: Blast radius measures failure impact: (Users Affected ÷ Total) × Duration. Minimizing blast radius means distributing across AZs/regions, isolating failure domains, using bulkheads, and gradual rollouts — so one failure affects as few users as possible.

questions.question: In Domain-Driven Design (DDD), what is a 'Bounded Context'?
options: A database table containing domain entities
An explicit boundary within which a domain model is defined and applicable
A microservice with limited CPU resources
A class with restricted public methods
questions.explanation: A Bounded Context is an explicit boundary within which a particular domain model is defined and consistent. The same concept (e.g., 'Customer') can have different meanings in different bounded contexts (Sales vs Shipping vs Billing) without conflict.

questions.question: In DDD, what is an 'Aggregate Root'?
options: The root module in a monorepo
The single entry point to a cluster of related objects that enforces consistency boundaries
The top-level class in an inheritance hierarchy
The primary key of the most important database table
questions.explanation: An Aggregate Root is the single entity through which all operations on an aggregate cluster must pass. It enforces invariants and consistency rules. External objects can only reference the root, not internal entities — ensuring business rules remain intact.

questions.question: What is the primary advantage of the Hexagonal Architecture (Ports & Adapters) pattern?
options: It makes code run faster
It isolates the domain logic from infrastructure, making it testable without real databases or frameworks
It provides automatic scaling
It reduces the number of classes needed
questions.explanation: Hexagonal Architecture separates the domain core (business logic) from infrastructure (databases, HTTP, messaging) through ports (interfaces) and adapters (implementations). The domain can be tested in isolation — no real database needed.

questions.question: What is the Richardson Maturity Model Level 2 for REST APIs, and why is it considered the practical 'sweet spot'?
options: Level 2 uses HATEOAS links for full discoverability — most REST APIs achieve this
Level 2 uses proper HTTP verbs and status codes — enough structure without the complexity of HATEOAS
Level 2 requires binary serialization for performance
Level 2 only supports GET and POST methods
questions.explanation: Richardson Level 2 uses proper HTTP verbs (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE) and meaningful status codes (200, 201, 404, 409). Level 3 adds HATEOAS (hypermedia links) which adds complexity rarely justified in practice — Level 2 is the pragmatic sweet spot.

questions.question: What are the key advantages of gRPC over REST with JSON?
options: gRPC is easier to use from browsers and requires no code generation
gRPC uses binary serialization (3-10x smaller), HTTP/2, streaming, and type-safe code generation
gRPC has better support for public APIs and documentation
gRPC works without a schema definition
questions.explanation: gRPC uses Protocol Buffers (binary, 3-10x smaller than JSON), HTTP/2 (multiplexing, bidirectional streaming), and generates type-safe client/server code. Avoid it for browser clients and public APIs where REST's human-readability matters more.

questions.question: What is 'emergent behavior' in a distributed system?
options: Behavior explicitly coded into the system
Behavior arising from the interactions of many simple local rules, not predictable from any single component
Errors that emerge only in production
Performance that emerges after warm-up periods
questions.explanation: Emergent behavior arises from simple local rules applied by many independent agents — not explicitly programmed at the system level. Traffic phantom jams emerge from individual following-distance rules. Retry storms emerge from per-service timeout logic.

questions.question: In microservices, services should share a single database to ensure data consistency across the system.
questions.explanation: False. Sharing a database between microservices creates tight coupling — schema changes in one service break others, and you lose independent deployability. Each microservice should own its data. Consistency across services is achieved through eventual consistency patterns (events, sagas).
